doc.title: Basic EPS
doc.event: Gray Corp. reports net income of $550,000. Outstanding: 10,000 shares of 6%, $100-par preferred and common stock with weighted-average shares of 200,000. Compute basic EPS.

variants.label: Cumulative — deduct the full preference, declared or not

computationPaths.narration: Basic EPS = income AVAILABLE TO COMMON ÷ weighted-average common shares. Cumulative preferred's current-year preference comes off the top whether or not the board declared it — the claim accumulates either way. (Arrears from PRIOR years never re-deduct; each year deducts only its own preference.)

variants.label: Noncumulative and undeclared — no deduction at all

computationPaths.narration: Noncumulative preferred only claims what's DECLARED. Nothing declared → nothing deducted → all $550,000 belongs to common.

memorize.body: Basic EPS = (Net income − preferred dividends) ÷ weighted-average common shares. Cumulative: deduct the current-year preference always. Noncumulative: deduct only if declared.
Deduct ONE year of cumulative preference — not the arrears pile. Prior years' preferences reduced THEIR years' EPS already.
The denominator is the WASO you built in the previous scenario — the two computations chain: WASO feeds basic, basic feeds diluted.

questions.prompt: NI 550,000; cumulative 6% preferred, $1,000,000 total par, NOT declared this year; WASO 200,000. Basic EPS =

distractors.feedback: 2.75 skips the preferred deduction because nothing was declared — but CUMULATIVE preference deducts regardless of declaration. (550,000 − 60,000) ÷ 200,000 = 2.45.
